title: Making your own die-cuts, templates and hand cutting for scrapbooking
subtitle: How to produce repeatable cut shapes without a die-cutting machine
excerpt: Die-cuts are shaped pieces of paper or card cut to a precise outline and used as decorative elements in scrapbook layouts. Without a die-cutting machine, the same results are achievable by making a reusable card template and cutting around it by hand. This article covers template making, tracing, and cutting clean shapes from multiple sheets.

glossaryTerms.definition: A paper or card shape cut precisely to a specific outline using either a mechanical die press or a hand-cut template. Die-cuts are used as decorative elements in scrapbooks, cards, and journalling.

A reusable cutting guide made from stiff card or acetate. The template is traced around with a pencil each time, allowing identical shapes to be cut from multiple sheets without redrawing the outline.

content.text: that holds its shape through repeated use. Cereal box card is too thin and distorts quickly. The ideal template material is a piece of stiff bookbinding board or several layers of card laminated with PVA, firm enough that a pencil tracing around it produces a consistent line every time.

attrs.title: Craft knife safety
attrs.body: Always cut away from fingers and use a metal ruler to guide straight cuts. Keep the blade sharp, a dull blade requires more pressure and is more likely to skip across the paper. Replace blades when they begin to drag. Keep fingers behind the blade line at all times.

items.name: Stiff card for template
items.notes: Bookbinding board at 1.5-2 mm works well. Alternatively, three layers of standard card laminated with PVA and dried flat under a weight.

items.name: Decorative paper or card for cutting
items.notes: The material the finished die-cuts will be made from. Can be patterned, textured, or plain.

items.name: Craft knife with fresh blade
items.notes: For cutting curved shapes, a smaller blade (no. 11) offers more control than a standard blade.

items.name: Self-healing cutting mat, A4
items.notes: Essential for protecting the work surface and keeping cuts clean.

items.name: Pencil
items.notes: For tracing the template outline onto the cutting sheet. Use a hard pencil (2H) for a thin, precise line.

content.text: Draw the intended shape onto the stiff card at the exact size required. Cut it out carefully with the craft knife on the cutting mat. This is the

: check that the edges are smooth and the outline is clean before using it, since any irregularity in the template will be reproduced in every cut made from it. Label the template on the back with the intended size and use.

Lay the template on the decorative sheet and trace around it lightly with a hard pencil. Remove the template, place the sheet on the cutting mat, and cut along the inside edge of the pencil line. Cutting on the inside of the line removes the pencil mark and produces a clean edge. For curved cuts, rotate the cutting mat rather than the knife hand, this maintains a consistent cutting angle around the curve.

efficiently, stack up to three sheets of the cutting material and trace the template once on the top sheet. Cut through all layers together, a sharp blade passes through three sheets of standard paper cleanly. More than three sheets causes the layers to shift and the lower cuts to be slightly inaccurate.
